perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "employees" shall include independent contractors of each party.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "employees" shall include only full-time staff of each party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of 'employees' to exclude independent contractors, the clause now contradicts the original intent. This introduces ambiguity because elsewhere in the document there is no change and it says 'The receiving party will not disclose any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any employees or to any third parties except to the receiving party’s employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries who have a need to know and who agree to abide by nondisclosure terms at least as comprehensive as those set forth herein; provided that the receiving party will be liable for breach by any such entity.'. So, this is creating an in-text contradiction by narrowing the scope of who is considered an employee.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations of Receiving Party. The receiving party will maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances. The receiving party will not disclose any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any employees or to any third parties except to the receiving party’s employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries who have a need to know and who agree to abide by nondisclosure terms at least as comprehensive as those set forth herein; provided that the receiving party will be liable for breach by any such entity. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "employees" shall include only full-time staff of each party. The receiving party will not make any copies of the Confidential Information received from the disclosing party except as necessary for its employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries with a need to know. Any copies, which are made, will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and marked "confidential", "proprietary" or with a similar legend.
perturbation.justification: The original text included independent contractors as 'employees'. The changed text narrows the definition of 'employees' to only full-time staff. The contradicted text refers to 'employees' without this new restrictive definition. This creates a direct conflict because the receiving party is now restricted from disclosing information to independent contractors under the original terms, while the amended text now excludes them from the employee definition. This contradicts the original intent regarding who is considered an employee for the purposes of non-disclosure, and creates an omission by potentially excluding certain entities from the confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will not make any copies of the Confidential Information received from the disclosing party except as necessary for its employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries with a need to know. Any copies, which are made, will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and marked "confidential", "proprietary" or with a similar legend.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will not make any copies of the Confidential Information received from the disclosing party except as absolutely necessary. Any copies, which are made, will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries with a need to know' and changing 'as necessary' to 'as absolutely necessary' and removing the clause 'marked “confidential”, “proprietary” or with a similar legend', this modified clause creates an in-text contradiction by tightening the conditions for copying the data without specifying who can make the copies anymore and also the markings. This contradicts the reasonable expectation that copying may be needed within the organization, making it uncertain when copying is allowed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations of Receiving Party. The receiving party will maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances. The receiving party will not disclose any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any employees or to any third parties except to the receiving party’s employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries who have a need to know and who agree to abide by nondisclosure terms at least as comprehensive as those set forth herein; provided that the receiving party will be liable for breach by any such entity. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "employees" shall include independent contractors of each party. The receiving party will not make any copies of the Confidential Information received from the disclosing party except as absolutely necessary. Any copies, which are made, will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly tightens the conditions for copying confidential information by removing the specific categories of individuals ('employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries with a need to know') who were previously authorized to make copies. This contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which outlines the 'Obligations of Receiving Party' and states that the receiving party *can* disclose confidential information to the receiving party's employees, parent company, affiliates, and subsidiaries who have a need to know. By removing the specific group that can receive copies and tightening the requirement to 'as absolutely necessary', the changed text creates a direct conflict, leading to uncertainty about when and to whom confidential information can be copied and creates an in-text contradiction according to the provided definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall bind the Parties for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information, unless any of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 below occurs.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall bind the Parties indefinitely, unless any of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 below occurs.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality period from 'five (5) years' to 'indefinitely' creates an in-text contradiction given common business practices. It implies an unusually long-term commitment to confidentiality, which could conflict with the 'Exceptions to the Obligation of Confidentiality' outlined in Section 4, particularly clause (a) concerning information publicly disclosed. Making it indefinite when section 4(a) mentions 'generally made available publicly' creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Exceptions to the Obligation of Confidentiality. The receiving party will not be liable for the disclosure of any Confidential Information which is: (a) generally made available publicly or to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on disclosure; (b) rightfully received from a third party without any obligation of confidentiality; (c) rightfully known to the receiving party without any limitation on disclosure prior to its receipt from the disclosing party; (d) independently developed by employees and / or independent contactors of the receiving party; or (e) required to be disclosed in accordance with applicable laws, regulations, court, judicial or other government order, provided that the receiving party shall give the disclosing party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order.
perturbation.justification: The change from a 'five (5) years' confidentiality period to 'indefinitely' directly contradicts Section 4(a) of the 'Exceptions to the Obligation of Confidentiality'. Section 4(a) states that the receiving party is not liable for disclosure if the confidential information is 'generally made available publicly'. An indefinite confidentiality obligation is inherently inconsistent with information that is publicly available, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may, at any time: (a) cease giving Confidential Information to the other party without any liability, and/or (b) request in writing the return or destruction of all or part of its Confidential Information previously disclosed, and all copies thereof, and the receiving party will promptly comply with such request, and certify in writing its compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may, at any time: (a) cease giving Confidential Information to the other party without any liability.
perturbation.explanation: By removing clause (b) concerning the return or destruction of confidential information, the agreement now lacks a mechanism for the disclosing party to reclaim its information. The implication is that while the parties can stop sharing information, the receiving party retains all previously disclosed information indefinitely. This contradicts the principle of confidentiality by potentially allowing the receiving party to use the information even after the disclosing party wishes it to be returned and destroyed creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: No Obligation of Disclosure; Termination. Neither party has any obligation to disclose Confidential Information to the other. Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party; provided that each party’s obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination. Either party may, at any time: (a) cease giving Confidential Information to the other party without any liability.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for both ceasing the giving of confidential information and requesting its return/destruction. The changed text removes the clause regarding return/destruction. The contradicted text contains the clause enabling termination of the agreement, stipulating that obligations related to confidential information survive termination. By removing the return/destruction clause, the changed text creates a scenario where the receiving party might retain confidential information indefinitely, even after the agreement is terminated and the disclosing party wants it back. This directly contradicts the implication in the contradicted text that obligations regarding confidential information survive termination, as the disclosing party now lacks a clear mechanism to reclaim its information. The omission therefore results in an in-text contradiction, especially concerning the enforcement and interpretation of confidentiality obligations post-termination, which falls under 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.
